title: Bound For The Lord-Knows-Where
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Where are you going with your horse and bike,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the townsfolk still at rest?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where are you going, with your swag and pack,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the night still in the West?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your clothes are worn, and your cheques are gone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But your eyes are free from care?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weן¿½re bushmen down for a spree in town,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And weן¿½re bound for the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old chap, weן¿½re bound for the Lord-knows-where.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (There are great dark scrubs in the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where they fight it out alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are wide wide plains in the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where a manן¿½s soul is his own.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is healthy work, there is healthy rest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is peace from self-torture there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the glorious freedom from paltriness!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And theyן¿½re bound for the Lord-knows-where.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now, where are you going in your Sunday suit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a bag for your second best?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now where are you going with your chest of tools,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the old togs in the chest?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With your six clean shirts and a pound of ן¿½weedן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And enough for a third-class fare?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh! Iן¿½ll be afloat by the very next boat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½m bound for the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old chap, Iן¿½m bound for the Lord-knows-where.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (There are wide wide seas to the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where a man might have a spell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The things turn up in the Lord-knows-where that
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We waited for too well.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a stranger land in the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a show for the stranger there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is war and quake more work to make,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heן¿½s bound for the Lord-knows-where.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now where are you going with your Gladstone bag,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With your shirt-case and valise?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now where are you going with your cap and shoes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your looks of joyful peace?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now where are you going with your money belts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your drafts on the first bank there?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½We have made a hit,ן¿½ or ן¿½weן¿½ve made a bit,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And weן¿½re bound for the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old chap, weן¿½re bound for the Lord-knows-where.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (There are sinful ports in the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are marvellous sights to see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are high old games in the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That were known to you and me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is love and music, and life and light from
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Heads to ן¿½Lesterן¿½ Square,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is more than space for their high young hearts
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is safety or danger there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And theyן¿½ll come back wild, or theyן¿½ll come back tamed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When theyן¿½ve been to the Lord-knows-where.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now where am I going with my whisky flask,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with little else beside?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now where am I going with my second shirt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To wear while the first is dried?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have marred my name, and Iן¿½ve lost my fame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But my hopeן¿½s in good repair.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are lies about, there are warrants out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½m bound for the Lord-knows-where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Chap, and Iן¿½m bound for the Lord-knows-where.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Thereן¿½s a rise and fall of the sloping decks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That is good for a soul in pain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s the drowsy rest on the sunlight sea
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till your strength comes back again.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, the wild mad spirit is hypnotized,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And nerves are tranquil there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the past is hushed in forgetfulness,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the road to the Lord-knows-where.)
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
